1: יַעַנְךָ יְהוָה בְּיוֹם צָרָה (yaʿankā YHWH bĕyôm ṣārāh, "May the LORD answer you in your day of trouble!") — the jussive yaʿankā (may he answer you) opens a series of blessings for the king before battle. The congregation speaks in the third person about the king and the LORD. Yôm ṣārāh: the day of tight distress (same root as ṣar, Psalms 4:1; Psalms 18:6). יְשַׂגֶּבְךָ שֵׁם אֱלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב (yĕśaggĕbkā shêm ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb, "May the name of the God of Jacob set you on high!") — śāgab (piel: to set on high, to make inaccessible/safe): the name of the God of Jacob as the one who lifts up to safety. The title "God of Jacob" invokes the patriarchal covenant.
2: יִשְׁלַח-עֶזְרְךָ מִקֹּדֶשׁ (yishlah-ʿezrĕkā miqqōdesh, "May he send you help from his sanctuary") — the divine help is dispatched (shālaḥ: to send, to release) from the sanctuary (qōdesh): the heavenly temple, the source of all aid. וּמִצִּיּוֹן יִסְעָדֶךָּ (ûmîṣṣiyyôn yisʿādekā, "support you from Zion!") — sāʿad: to support, sustain (as one supports another who is about to fall); Zion as the earthly location of divine presence.
3: יִזְכֹּר כָּל-מִנְחֹתֶיךָ (yizkōr kol-minḥōtêkā, "May he remember all your offerings") — zākar: to remember with active concern, to call to mind and act on. The minḥāh (grain offering) and ʿôlāh (burnt offering — what goes up in smoke entirely) as the worshiping acts that precede battle. The prayer is that God accepts and responds to these acts of worship. Selāh marks the pause after the first unit of blessing.
4: יִתֶּן-לְךָ כִלְבָבֶךָ (yittĕn-lĕkā kilbābĕkā, "May he grant you your heart's desire") — the prayer for divine alignment with the king's desires — not a blank check but the prayer that the king's desires are already aligned with God's will (implied by the preceding religious context). וְכָל-עֲצָתְךָ יְמַלֵּא (wĕkol-ʿăṣātĕkā yĕmallêʾ, "and fulfill all your plans!") — ʿēṣāh: counsel, plan, strategy. Mālêʾ (piel: to fill, to fulfill, to bring to completion).
5: נְרַנְּנָה בִּישׁוּעָתֶךָ (nĕrannĕnāh bîshûʿātekā, "May we shout for joy at your victory") — rānan: to shout, to ring out (a ringing cry of joy or lamentation — context determines which); here the shout of victory. The congregation anticipates celebrating the king's salvation as their own. וּבְשֵׁם-אֱלֹהֵינוּ נִדְגֹּל (ûbĕshêm-ʾĕlōhênû nidgōl, "in the name of our God raise our banners!") — dāgal: to raise a banner/standard; the military banner as the visible rallying point. Raising banners in God's name rather than in the king's name or the nation's.
6: עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי כִּי הוֹשִׁיעַ יְהוָה מְשִׁיחוֹ (ʿattāh yādaʿtî kî hôshîaʿ YHWH mĕshîḥô, "Now I know that the LORD saves his anointed one") — the singular first person breaks in: "now I know." Whether a priest, prophet, or the king himself speaking, this is a declaration of certainty. Yādaʿ (to know): experiential, relational knowing. יַעֲנֵהוּ מִשְּׁמֵי קָדְשׁוֹ (yaʿănêhû mishshĕmê qodshô, "he answers him from his holy heaven") — the answer comes from the holy heavens (shĕmê qodshô: heavens of his holiness), not from earthly resources. Bighĕbûrôt yêshaʿ yĕmînô: "by the saving power (gĕbûrôt, mighty acts/power) of his right hand."
7: אֵלֶּה בָרֶכֶב וְאֵלֶּה בַסּוּסִים (ʾêlleh bārekeb wĕʾêlleh bassûsîm, "Some boast in chariots and some in horses") — the contrast is absolute: the military establishment of Israel's neighbors depends on chariot-forces and cavalry (the dominant military technology of the ancient Near East). וַאֲנַחְנוּ בְּשֵׁם-יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ נַזְכִּיר (waʾănaḥnû bĕshêm-YHWH ʾĕlōhênû nazkîr, "but we will boast in the name of the LORD our God") — zākar (hiphil: to make remembered, to invoke, to call to mind): not merely "mention" but actively invoke the name as the basis of confidence.
8: הֵמָּה כָּרְעוּ וְנָפָלוּ (hêmmāh kāreʿû wĕnāpālû, "They collapse and fall") — the confident perfect tense of certainty: it is as if already accomplished. Those who trust chariots (kāraʿ: bow, bend the knee — in defeat; nāpal: fall) will fail. וַאֲנַחְנוּ קַּמְנוּ וַנִּתְעוֹדָד (waʾănaḥnû qamnû wannitʿôdād, "but we rise and stand firm") — qûm (to rise) and ʿûd (hithpael: to strengthen oneself, to rally): those who trust in the divine name rise and are strengthened.
9: יְהוָה הוֹשִׁיעָה (YHWH hôshîʿāh, "LORD, give victory to the king!") — the hiphil imperative of yāshaʿ: save! The abrupt imperative after vv6-8's confident declarations is the cry that gives voice to the underlying dependence. The psalm ends as a prayer rather than a triumphant statement: confidence is founded on divine saving, so the final word is a request for exactly that. הַמֶּלֶךְ יַעֲנֵנוּ בְיוֹם-קָרְאֵנוּ (hammelekh yaʿănênû bĕyôm-qārĕʾênû, "Answer us when we call") — the MT is difficult: "the King will answer us in the day we call" — may be a statement of confidence rather than a petition, reading yaʿănênû as a declarative rather than a jussive.
